patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch limits the number of iterations for shrink_delalloc in Btrfs to prevent potential deadlocks or system stalls, and is part of a larger series that has been reverted due to unforeseen issues.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo proposed a patch that adds a condition to set nr_pages to 1 when it is zero in the shrink_delalloc function, citing that this solves the dead looping kworker on arm64 issue but does not fix the test case itself which still runs indefinitely. They believe the old 3 loops limit is what makes the difference and consider their solution 'ugly' but effective.
review_comments.raw_body:
I go with the following diff:

diff --git a/fs/btrfs/space-info.c b/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
index e931deb3d013..2c5214b24239 100644
--- a/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
+++ b/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
@@ -770,6 +770,9 @@ static void shrink_delalloc(struct btrfs_space_info 
*space_info,
                 u64 items = calc_reclaim_items_nr(fs_info, 
iter_reclaim) * 2;
                 int async_pages;

+               if (nr_pages == 0)
+                       nr_pages = 1;
+
                 btrfs_start_delalloc_roots(fs_info, nr_pages, true);

                 /*


It solves the dead looping kworker on arm64, now the it's several 
different kworkers taking around 5~15% along with 027 itself taking CPU 
time.

But unfortunately the test case itself still seems not to end any time soon.

I believe the old 3 loops limit is really what makes the difference.
It's ugly, but at least it seems to work.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that their original patch contained an unbounded loop and a min() bug, which caused the system to get stuck waiting on a commit. They expressed regret for including such code and implied they will rework the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for re-testing. Glad to hear that at least fixes the dead loop.

Honestly I should have known better than to include an unbounded loop,
that was stupid. I even kind of thought it was dumb while doing it but
convinced myself it "must make progress one extent at a time" or
whatever.. Obviously overlooked the min() bug too.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that in extreme ENOSPC situations, the delalloc shrinking loop can get stuck due to a semi-deadlock between metadata reservation and folio writeback, particularly when large folios are involved, and suggested adding a proper way to break out of the loop when no progress is made.
review_comments.raw_body:
Not an expert on the space reclaim part, but as far as I understand, 
there can be some cases where the only dirty folios that can be flushed 
is the folios that are being written back.

E.g. we have a large folio (256KiB) dirty, and that's the only dirty 
data folio in the whole fs.

Then at delalloc time, we are reserving metadata, and we are waiting for 
the ticket to shrink delalloc.

Although the ticket is trying to flush delalloc, the 256KiB dirty folio 
is already under write back, thus it will reclaim no space.
Thus the loop never progresses.

This will cause a semi-deadlock, as we want to reserve metadata to do 
the folio writeback, but the metadata reserving is trying to flush the 
same folio to progress.

And the recent huge folio feature is making the problem more obvious, as 
the folios grow larger, we have higher chance to hit such situation.


With that said, it means delalloc shrinking is never guaranteed to make 
any progress under extreme ENOSPC situation.
Thus a proper way to break out the loop is highly recommended, when 
there is no progress to be done.

Hopes this educated guess would help to explain the problem.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that on their arm64 machine, the first three patches are not the root cause of the issue, but the last patch is still causing excessive CPU usage and consistently hangs the system within a certain time limit.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that on arm64 systems during g/027 test, the CPU usage is consistently high (~100%) on a kworker process, which was used as the bisecting criteria due to its reliability and speed.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the kworker process is experiencing 100% CPU usage due to what appears to be a deadlock loop, indicating a significant performance issue.

Qu Wenruo reported that the patch series causes a hang on their system, and provided a call trace showing a deadlock between btrfs_async_reclaim_metadata_space and _raw_spin_unlock_irqrestore. They suspect that excessive space reclaim/pinning reclaim is choking the workload due to excessive reservation.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed understanding for the challenges of ENOSPC in btrfs, but prefers to revert the last commit to continue regular testing.

root problem.

In fact on v7.0-rc7, I can still hit generic/224 hang, aka kernel 
detects 120s time out for hung processes, and my VM is configured to 
reset after such detection.

I'm going to slightly loose the hung task detection time (120s->150s) 
and check if it's just too slow in this particular case.


Although the last patch is still causing excessive CPU usage here, and 
very reliably.

At least we got something that both of us can reproduce.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

version_history.subject: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes the SVC_POOL_* enum and mode selection heuristic, simplifying the pool map functions to always use the pernode path. The percpu mode is removed as it created excessive pools relative to the number of threads most deployments run. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility, but writing any value other than 'pernode' will return an error. The default pool mode is hardcoded to pernode, which provides one pool per node with proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever expressed concern that the patch hard-fails on legacy module parameter settings, instead recommending acceptance and ignoring of old values for backwards compatibility. He suggested changing the 'auto' mode to per-node and adding deprecation warnings before removal of other modes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Generally, I think the end goal of making "per-node" the default
and only setting is correct. My comments below are about how we
get there.

The main concern is not perturbing any configuration that today
happens to set the module parameter.

The proposed patch correctly preserves the shape of the user/kernel
interfaces (same module parameter name and perms, same netlink command
and attribute, same exported symbol names and signatures). The concern
is that the accepted input set has narrowed from four strings to one
and the setters reject the legacy three with -EINVAL. For the module
parameter that path runs at module load, so existing modprobe.d configs
written any time in the last ~19 years will cause load-time parameter
errors. Some might categorize that as a regression.
                                      
The commit message documents that non-"pernode" writes now return
-EINVAL. The historically correct approach for this kind of obsoleted
tuning knob is to accept-and-ignore the old values (plus the pr_notice)
rather than hard-fail.

Or, put another way, the proposed patch implements something slightly
different than true backwards compatibility. Userspace that previously
set pool_mode=global/percpu/auto now gets -EINVAL, which technically
speaking is a behavioral narrowing, not "backward compatibility."

I might go even further and suggest that perhaps for v7.2:

- Change the behavior of "auto" to be per-node
- Add a deprecation warning that is emitted when setting other modes,
  but don't warn when the value is specifically the only accepted one.

Then wait a few more cycles before removal of percpu and global.

Other notes:

o The existing pernode path assumes every online node has both CPUs and
  some memory. nr_online_nodes on some platforms (e.g., certain ARM64,
  CXL) counts memoryless or CPU-less nodes; for_each_node_with_cpus()
  vs. for_each_online_node() matters here. See
  svc_pool_map_init_pernode().

o Recommend review of some history on this topic:
  https://lore.kernel.org/linux-nfs/f027319a-378d-4b91-a418-c45218fb7a21@oracle.com/


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Chuck Lever's concern about the default pool_mode being 'global' instead of 'auto', explaining that 'pernode' is functionally identical to 'global' on single-NUMA hosts and 'percpu' mode is useless. Author suggests changing the default to 'pernode' initially as a compromise.

Author agrees that a warning is necessary when removing the percpu option, indicating a fix will be included in future revisions.

Author acknowledges that removing all pool modes except pernode is a significant change and agrees to stage it in slowly, but expresses concern about following up with later phases of deprecation.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's already the case if you're on a NUMA box. The problem is that
the default is not "auto" but "global". We could change that (easily),
but I'd argue that "auto" mode just devolves into "pernode" for two
reasons:

- "pernode" is functionally identical to "global" when there is only a
single NUMA node

- "percpu" mode is basically useless

If we want to "go slow" on this, then what I'd probably suggest is that
we just change the default to "pernode" initially.

Single node boxes that have this set to global today should see no real
change (functionally identical). Boxes with multiple NUMA nodes that
don't set it now, will start using pernode mode, but that's probably a
good thing in most cases.

---

We probably do still want to warn in that case. Once we remove the
option, the module load can fail, so we probably want to discourage
people from keeping the setting.

---

That's fair. We can stage this in slowly.

I'm terrible about following up with the later phases of long term
deprecation though. Suggestions on how to make sure we don't drop the
ball on finishing the change?

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the distinction between 'auto' and 'default' pool modes is unclear, describing it as 'whacky'.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that setting a deprecated pool mode should not cause module loading to fail, as failing to load would be more painful than loading and having different thread affinity behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, there seems to be a difference between "auto" and "default". That's
a little whacky.

---

I'm saying that setting a deprecated pool mode should not cause
module loading to fail at all. Failing to load will be more painful
than loading and having different thread affinity behavior.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 01/13] fuse: fix missing forget requests in error paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module where forget requests were not being sent to the server when it returned valid node IDs but invalid attributes, leading to potential server-side leaks. The patch modifies error paths in the fuse_lookup_name and fuse_create_open functions to send forget requests when necessary. This ensures that the server releases its reference on the file, preventing memory leaks. Although the real-world impact is minimal due to the specific conditions required for this bug to occur, sending forget requests is still essential for maintaining a healthy connection with the FUSE server.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes simplifying the logic for dispatching requests in the fuse io-uring implementation by using the group_cpus_evenly() function, which partitions CPUs based on numa nodes and other considerations. This would eliminate the need for per-numa state tracking and simplify queue selection to a simple cpu-to-qid lookup. Additionally, it suggests declaring the total queue count upfront and establishing the mapping at ring creation time, eliminating the rebuild-on-each-queue-creation complexity.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Bernd Schubert expressed skepticism about the proposed change, questioning whether it's necessary and suggesting alternative priorities, such as addressing lock issues and enabling eBPF override of queue selection.

Reviewer Bernd Schubert disagreed that the current approach could make everything entirely dynamic, instead suggesting a config SQE would make sense and proposing an alternative where libfuse starts with minimal memory usage and dynamically adds ring entries based on request size
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe, I need to check that code. However, does this really need to be
done right now? This cannot be updated later? For me it looks a bit like
we are going to replace one code by another, without a clear advantage.
I can look into group_cpus_evenly(), but I cannot promise you when that
will happen.
My personal preference would be to work on real issue, like getting rid
of two locks (queue->lock and bg->lock) and distribute max_bg accross
queues. And that probably requires the distribution across queues, which
you didn't like in the previous series. Anway, already finding the time
for that is hard.

My personal opinion is that queue selection needs to return the qid, so
that the function can be overriden with eBPF. I didn't have time yet to
try that out.

---

That is why I said in another mail that a config SQE would make to some
extend sense. However, the part where I disagree is that we could make
it all entirely dynamic with the current approach.
Only the logic for that in libfuse is missing. I.e. it _could_ start
with a single queue or one queue per numa and one ring entry. Basically
no memory usage then.
And now libfuse could add logic - many small requests - set up ring
entries with smaller payload size (or smaller pBuf). Many large requests
- add more requests with larger payload size. And with the current
approach queues can be added dynamically.


Thanks,
Bernd

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: preemptively free dead folios during lru_add drain

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: preemptively free dead folios during lru_add drain

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the Linux kernel's memory management by preemptively freeing dead folios during the lru_add process, which reduces lock contention and improves system performance. The patch identifies that a significant portion of observable lruvec lock contention occurs when dead folios are present in lru_add batches at drain time, leading to unnecessary lock acquisitions. To address this issue, the patch uses folio_ref_freeze() to filter out dead folios before they enter the LRU and moves them onto a temporary batch for deferred cleanup. The patch was tested on a production workload and showed significant improvements in system-wide memory stats, including reduced direct reclaim scanning, allocation stalls, compaction stalls, and page frees.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested moving the dead folio filtering outside of the lru lock, noting that a batched version is not currently available in vmscan.c and swap.c, but did not consider this a blocker for the current patch.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned why dead folios were being added to the LRU in the first place, suggesting that they may have been freed or had their last non-LRU-cache reference dropped during their stay in the cache.

Reviewer suggested adding a similar optimization to folio_add_lru(), questioning whether the current patch is sufficient
sentiment_signals: requested clarification on the behavior of dead folios
review_comments.raw_body:
So, when they were added to the percpu lru cache, they were alive but during
their stay in lru cache, they were freed (last non-lrucache ref dropped) or
somehow we are adding folio where the caller drops the reference just after
adding to percpu lru cache e.g. folio_putback_lru() ?

---

folio_add_lru() and if that would be enough.

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that both scenarios (callers putting folio while on per-cpu batch and callers dropping ref from 2 to 1) can occur, but emphasized the importance of the batch ref remaining.

The author addressed Shakeel Butt's concern that folio_add_lru() adds a dead folio to the batch, by explaining that the folio is still alive when added and only becomes dead later.
review_comments.raw_body:
Both scenarios can occur. Whether all callers put the folio while it is
on the per-cpu batch or putback drops ref from 2 to 1, the batch ref
is what remains.

[...]

---

folio_add_lru() is how it gets onto the batch. But it's still alive at
that point - at least one caller ref.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested avoiding folio_add_lru() by doing special checking at folio_putback_lru(), potentially reducing lock contention, but noted that this analysis can be done later.

Yeah, I was thinking if we do special checking at folio_putback_lru() to avoid
folio_add_lru() at all (only if folio_putback_lru() is the one causing such
scenario most of the time).

Anyways, that analysis can be done later. So, for the path:

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about implementing the dead folio filtering as a special case for MGLRU (Multi-Generation LRU), proposing to add additional code to handle this scenario and asking for thoughts on an alternative patch by Barry.
review_comments.raw_body:
Barry/Shakeel,

We can do something like this as a special case for MGLRU:

diff --git a/mm/swap.c b/mm/swap.c
index 71607b0ce3d18..68ea929f65031 100644
--- a/mm/swap.c
+++ b/mm/swap.c
@@ -185,6 +185,8 @@ static void folio_batch_move_lru(struct folio_batch 
*fbatch, move_fn_t move_fn)
  		 * deferred split list to avoid a dangling list entry.
  		 */
  		if (is_lru_add && folio_ref_freeze(folio, 1)) {
+			if (lru_gen_enabled())
+				__folio_clear_active(folio);
  			folio_unqueue_deferred_split(folio);
  			fbatch->folios[i] = NULL;
  			folio_batch_add(&free_fbatch, folio);

Unless Barry's patch works out... Any thoughts?

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Shakeel Butt noted that the patch can be removed later and suggested it should be addressed in a separate follow-up patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this is fine. We can remove this later when Barry's patch is settled in
a followup path.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/lruvec: preemptively free dead folios during lru_add drain
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: JP Kobryn proposed a patch to preemptively free dead folios during lru_add drain, but it's contingent on Barry's existing patch being resolved.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Expressed agreement with JP Kobryn's patch, but suggested removing it in favor of a follow-up path once Barry's patch is settled.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Apr 24, 2026 at 08:38:06AM -0700, JP Kobryn (Meta) wrote:
> On 4/23/26 4:53 PM, Barry Song wrote:
> > On Fri, Apr 24, 2026 at 7:46\u202fAM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
> > > 
> > > On Fri, Apr 24, 2026 at 07:22:30AM +0800, Barry Song wrote:
> > > > On Fri, Apr 24, 2026 at 12:43\u202fAM JP Kobryn (Meta) <jp.kobryn@linux.dev> wrote:
> > > > > 
> > > > > Of all observable lruvec lock contention in our fleet, we find that ~24%
> > > > > occurs when dead folios are present in lru_add batches at drain time. This
> > > > > is wasteful in the sense that the folio is added to the LRU just to be
> > > > > immediately removed via folios_put_refs(), incurring two unnecessary lock
> > > > > acquisitions.
> > > > > 
> > > > > Eliminate this overhead by preemptively cleaning up dead folios before they
> > > > > make it into the LRU. Use folio_ref_freeze() to filter folios whose only
> > > > > remaining refcount is the batch ref. When dead folios are found, move them
> > > > > off the add batch and onto a temporary batch to be freed.
> > > > > 
> > > > > During A/B testing on one of our prod instagram workloads (high-frequency
> > > > > short-lived requests), the patch intercepted almost all dead folios before
> > > > > they entered the LRU. Data collected using the mm_lru_insertion tracepoint
> > > > > shows the effectiveness of the patch:
> > > > > 
> > > > > Per-host LRU add averages at 95% CPU load
> > > > > (60 hosts each side, 3 x 60s intervals)
> > > > > 
> > > > >              dead folios/min  total folios/min   dead %
> > > > > unpatched:        1,297,785        19,341,986  6.7097%
> > > > > patched:                 14        19,039,996  0.0001%
> > > > > 
> > > > > Within this workload, we save ~2.6M lock acquisitions per minute per host
> > > > > as a result.
> > > > > 
> > > > > System-wide memory stats improved on the patched side also at 95% CPU load:
> > > > >   - direct reclaim scanning reduced 7%
> > > > >   - allocation stalls reduced 5.2%
> > > > >   - compaction stalls reduced 12.3%
> > > > >   - page frees reduced 4.9%
> > > > > 
> > > > > No regressions were observed in requests served per second or request tail
> > > > > latency (p99). Both metrics showed directional improvement at higher CPU
> > > > > utilization (comparing 85% to 95%).
> > > > > 
> > > > > Signed-off-by: JP Kobryn (Meta) <jp.kobryn@linux.dev>
> > > > 
> > > > Hi JP,
> > > > I\u2019m seeing a large number of "BAD page" bugs.
> > > > Not sure if it\u2019s related, but reverting this patch
> > > > seems to fix the issue.
> > > > 
> > > > [ 2869.365978] BUG: Bad page state in process uname  pfn:3a5417
> > > > [ 2869.365981] page: refcount:0 mapcount:0 mapping:0000000000000000
> > > > index:0x724884c20 pfn:0x3a5417
> > > > [ 2869.365983] flags:
> > > > 0x17ffffc0020908(uptodate|active|owner_2|swapbacked|node=0|zone=2|lastcpupid=0x1fffff)
> > > 
> > > Hi Barry, are you using MGLRU? It seems like MGLRU set active flag in
> > > folio_add_lru().
> > 
> > Yes. If you are referring to this set_active, I think it is
> > incorrect, so I have fixed it here and am waiting for review:
> >

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers allocated in current transaction

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers allocated in current transaction

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes Btrfs by skipping unnecessary copy-on-write (COW) operations when a buffer has been written in the current transaction. When memory pressure causes writeback of a recently COW'd buffer, Btrfs sets a flag indicating it's been written. Subsequent searches then re-COW the buffer unnecessarily, leading to performance degradation and potential exhaustion of block reservations. The patch addresses this issue by skipping COW for buffers allocated in the current transaction when they have the WRITTEN flag set and their generation matches the current transaction. This is safe because overwriting in place is crash-safe due to the committed superblock not referencing buffers allocated in the current transaction.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: don't finalize fs roots during qgroup snapshot accounting

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: don't finalize fs roots during qgroup snapshot accounting

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to avoid finalizing file system roots during qgroup snapshot accounting, which can lead to incorrect state and silent corruption of snapshots. The patch introduces a new helper function `prepare_root_item` that updates root items without the side effects of finalization, and a new function `qgroup_commit_fs_roots` that iterates over dirty roots using an advancing index instead of clearing tags. This change prevents premature clearing of `BTRFS_ROOT_FORCE_COW`, freeing of log trees, and other finalization actions during transaction progress, ensuring consistent qgroup accounting and preventing potential corruption issues.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/2] btrfs: skip COW for written extent buffers

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series optimizes Btrfs by skipping redundant copy-on-write (COW) operations on extent buffers that have already been written back to disk due to memory pressure. The optimization relies on the WRITTEN flag set by writeback, which indicates that a buffer has been committed to disk and can be safely overwritten in place. This reduces COW amplification, which can exhaust block reservations and degrade throughput under memory pressure. The patches also fix a latent issue in qgroup snapshot accounting, where root items were being persisted mid-transaction, leading to incorrect state and potential corruption. The skip-COW optimization is applied when should_cow_block() encounters a WRITTEN buffer from the current transaction, re-dirtying the buffer instead of allocating a new block.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't force DIO writes to be serialized

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't force DIO writes to be serialized

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where Direct I/O (DIO) writes are being serialized due to a missing flag that was set before the transition to the new mount API in 2023. The flag, SB_NOSEC, tells the VFS that the filesystem may have files without security xattrs, allowing optimizations. Without this flag, DIO writes to the same file will be locked exclusively, leading to reduced performance. This patch removes the serialization by not forcing DIO writes to be exclusive locks, resulting in a significant improvement in DIO throughput, as demonstrated by fio benchmark results showing a 59% increase.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David Sterba requested more information about the fio script and the block group profile used for DIO writes, indicating a need for additional context before fully evaluating the patch.

review_comments.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges a significant drop in DIO write performance in Linux 6.8, which was not recovered from, and plans to investigate automated performance testing to prevent similar issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
It is. There's a big dropoff in DIO write performance in 6.8 that we 
never recovered from. I'm going to look into some sort of automated 
performance so this kind of thing can't happen casually.

This was on a VM with 8 cores and 8GB of RAM, with a real NVMe exposed 
through PCI passthrough. The figures for XFS and ext4 in comparison are 
both about ~3GB/s.

# cat go
#!/bin/bash
mkfs.btrfs -f /dev/nvme0n1
mount /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/test
mkdir /mnt/test/nocow
chattr +C /mnt/test/nocow
fio /root/test.fio

# cat /root/test.fio
[global]
rw=randwrite
ioengine=io_uring
iodepth=64
size=1g
direct=1
startdelay=20
force_async=4
ramp_time=5
runtime=60
group_reporting=1
numjobs=32
time_based
disk_util=0
clat_percentiles=0
disable_lat=1
disable_clat=1
disable_slat=1
filename=/mnt/test/nocow/fiofile
[test]
name=test
bs=4k
stonewall

Qu

That sounds like a different thing - this is just making it so that
we're not forced to take the inode rwsem exclusively for each write.

> fighting with some memsw issue. I changed the semantic of the
> memory.swap counter a bit, but that makes it diverge operationally
> from memsw. Need to be careful not double charging or double
> uncharging here.

Hi Nhat,

No worries.

About memory.swap, I also thought about changing it a bit previously
to make thing cleaner:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260220-swap-table-p4-v1-7-104795d19815@tencent.com/

But I dropped that part so swap table series p4 can be reviewed
without any behavior change. Not sure if related.

> > I think it's actually functionally very similar to Nhat's design
> > already from a high level, the only difference is we don't need
> > standalone infra for virtual parts.
> Well yeah, great minds think alike ;)

:)

>
> As you have noticed, I have also converged towards a lot of your
> metadata design and operational arrangement.
>
> Case in point is the delaying of cgroup check merging with swap
> freeing - I did not notice that patch you had in your series, but I
> realized I had to do it as well after studying the regression for
> awhile.
>
> (I did think about proposing that outside of the vswap series, but I
> was thinking it would not be a problem at all with the current code.
> But in hindsight, since you're also merging swap cgroup with swap
> table, it will have a similar implications, albeit less expensive due
> to no xarray indirection).

There are actually more issues behind that. Later we would also want
to distinguish shmem / anon swap entries again, so swap cache
allocation ensures there is no conflict between these two. That is
also causing some real issues.

>
> Hopefully we can iron out the rest of the differences. I have a couple

Yeah, definitely. I think things are already getting much cleaner as
we keep unifying and simplify the swap infra. Consider the current
swap file something like s swap mapping as an infra seems to make
things easier to understand as well.

> more use cases in mind (compressed writeback from zswap, discontiguous
> fallback for swapout, etc.), but without virtualization they seem like

Ah, I'm not against a virtual layer at all, actually that's a shared
